title: Here's the Smartest Way to Invest in the S&P 500 in May
author: David Dierking
description: After market volatility in early 2026, the S&P 500 has recovered to all-time highs. Among four major S&P 500 ETFs, the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) is recommended as the best choice due to its combination of low expense ratio (0.03%), high liquidity, and large asset base that minimizes trading costs, despite SPYM having a slightly lower expense ratio.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Recommended as the best choice for S&P 500 investment due to optimal combination of low expense ratio (0.03%), highest trading volume and AUM ($916B), which results in lowest total costs including trading spreads despite not having the absolute lowest expense ratio.
Comparable to VOO with identical 0.03% expense ratio and similar performance metrics, but slightly lower AUM ($789B) and trading volume, making it marginally less advantageous on a total cost basis.
Explicitly dropped from consideration due to significantly higher expense ratio of 0.0945%, charging nearly 6.5 basis points more than competitors, making it uncompetitive for retail investors.
Has the lowest expense ratio at 0.02%, but lower AUM ($136B) and trading volume result in higher trading spreads, offsetting the expense ratio advantage on a total cost basis.

title: Same Index, Lower Fees: How SPLG Stacks Up Against SPY
author: Eric Trie
description: SPLG and SPY are both S&P 500 index ETFs with identical performance, but SPLG offers a lower expense ratio of 0.02% compared to SPY's 0.09%, making it more cost-efficient for long-term investors.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: High trading volume and liquidity, but higher expense ratio compared to SPLG
Mentioned as a top holding in both ETFs, no specific performance commentary

title: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF vs. SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF: One Offers Scale While the Other Boasts Lower Fees
author: Katie Brockman
description: Two S&P 500 ETFs from iShares and SPDR offer nearly identical performance, with subtle differences in expense ratios and assets under management. Both provide broad exposure to large-cap U.S. equities with minimal variation in sector weights and returns.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Large assets under management ($701.37 billion), long 25-year history, typical S&P 500 composition, and minimal expense ratio of 0.03%
Slightly lower expense ratio of 0.02%, similar performance to iShares ETF, and provides diversified access to large-cap U.S. equities
